expectations.text: Response uses contractions (e.g. you're, it's, don't, can't, won't)
expectations.evidence: Multiple contractions present: "you're probably using it", "It's also useful", "Don't use it on shared history", "haven't been shared yet", "others're working on", "editor'll open", "Git'll replay"

expectations.text: Response uses at least one informal reduction (gonna, wanna, kinda, etc.)
expectations.evidence: No informal reductions found. The response uses contractions but no 'gonna', 'wanna', 'kinda', or similar informal reductions.

expectations.text: Response uses full sentences — no dropped articles or caveman fragments
expectations.evidence: All prose uses complete sentences with articles intact: "Your editor'll open with a list like this", "Save the file, close the editor, and Git'll replay the commits", "That's fine for commits that haven't been shared yet"

expectations.text: Technical content about interactive rebase is accurate and complete
expectations.evidence: Covers all six commands (pick, reword, squash, fixup, edit, drop) with accurate descriptions, explains the interactive editor workflow, motivates usage with practical examples (squashing WIP commits), and correctly warns against rewriting shared history.

expectations.text: Articles (a/an/the) are largely absent from prose
expectations.evidence: Articles dropped throughout: "Map/object grows forever, never trimmed", "Each request adds entry, nothing evicts", "Heap climbs linearly with traffic", "Closure captures large obj/buffer", "Outer fn returns", "Take snapshot", "Generate load", "Start with `--inspect` flag"

expectations.text: Response uses contractions (don't, can't, it's, etc.)
expectations.evidence: "GC can't collect", "don't ignore it", "Heap profiler won't catch it"

expectations.text: Response uses short synonyms or fragment patterns typical of caveman mode
expectations.evidence: "Outer fn returns" (fn for function), "Fastest way to find *where* memory allocated", "Growing `external` or `arrayBuffers` = native addon or `Buffer` leak", "Steady climb = leak. Plateau then GC = not leak"

expectations.text: Covers at least 2 common causes of Node.js memory leaks
expectations.evidence: Covers 8 causes: unbounded caches/global accumulation, event emitter listeners never removed, closures holding refs, timers/intervals not cleared, streams not consumed/destroyed, forgotten promise chains, module-level arrays as queues, native addons/external memory.

expectations.text: Mentions at least one concrete debugging tool or technique
expectations.evidence: Multiple tools mentioned: clinic.js (`npx clinic heapprofiler`), V8 inspector via `--inspect` and chrome://inspect, `--expose-gc` for manual GC isolation, `memwatch-next` package, `process.memoryUsage()` polling.

expectations.text: Comments use the L<line>: format with file reference where applicable
expectations.evidence: All comments use L<line>: format: "L14:", "L15:", "L18-21:", "L19:", "L20:" — file reference to auth.js is implied by context and the L18-21 comment explicitly states "this is `auth.js`".

expectations.text: The null-access bug on user.profile.email is flagged
expectations.evidence: L15: "`user` can be null if `findOne` returns nothing; `.profile.email` throws. Guard before access."

expectations.text: At least one severity prefix (🔴/🟡/🔵/❓) is used
expectations.evidence: All three severity prefixes used: 🔵 (nit), 🟡 (risk), 🔴 (bug) — five comments total each with a prefix.

expectations.text: No throat-clearing phrases like 'I noticed that' or 'you might want to consider'
expectations.evidence: No throat-clearing phrases present. Comments are direct and action-oriented: "Guard before access", "Remove or replace with a proper logger", "Any caller can delete any user by id".

expectations.text: Each comment is a single line or very tightly formatted
expectations.evidence: Every comment is exactly one line: "L14: 🔵 nit: ...", "L15: 🟡 risk: ...", "L18-21: 🔴 bug: ...", "L19: 🟡 risk: ...", "L20: 🔵 nit: ..."

expectations.text: Response uses → for causality relationships
expectations.evidence: "One rejects → whole rejects immediately", "Use `all` → need all succeed, bail on first fail", "Use `allSettled` → need all results regardless of failures"

expectations.text: Response is noticeably more compressed than full mode would produce
expectations.evidence: Entire response is 7 lines with no code examples, no headers, no tables. Compare to the without_skill response which is ~60 lines with code blocks and a comparison table.

expectations.text: Contractions are present
expectations.evidence: No contractions found in the response. The text uses "Never rejects", "waits all settle", "need all succeed" — all contraction-free.

expectations.text: The fail-fast behavior of Promise.all is explained correctly
expectations.evidence: "fail-fast. One rejects → whole rejects immediately" — correctly captures that a single rejection causes immediate rejection of the whole.

expectations.text: The status/value/reason shape of allSettled results is mentioned
expectations.evidence: "Returns `[{status, value}]` or `[{status, reason}]` per promise" — correctly identifies both the fulfilled shape (status+value) and rejected shape (status+reason).

expectations.text: Response uses contractions (e.g. you're, it's, don't, can't, won't)
expectations.evidence: "Don't use it on commits that have already been pushed to a shared branch" — 'Don't' is present. Only one contraction found, making this a marginal pass.

expectations.text: Response uses at least one informal reduction (gonna, wanna, kinda, etc.)
expectations.evidence: No informal reductions found. Response is entirely formal prose with no 'gonna', 'wanna', 'kinda', or similar reductions.

expectations.text: Response uses full sentences — no dropped articles or caveman fragments
expectations.evidence: Fully formal sentences with articles throughout: "Git opens your configured editor with a list of the 3 most recent commits", "The core motivation is keeping a clean, readable commit history"

expectations.text: Technical content about interactive rebase is accurate and complete
expectations.evidence: Covers all interactive rebase commands (pick, reword, edit, squash, fixup, drop), explains the workflow, lists common use cases (squash, reword, reorder, drop, edit), and correctly warns against rewriting shared history.

expectations.text: Articles (a/an/the) are largely absent from prose
expectations.evidence: Articles are present throughout: "The most frequent culprit", "A `Map` or plain object used as a cache", "A closure keeps the entire scope chain alive", "In a long-running service", "The buffer accumulates" — full formal prose, articles not dropped.

expectations.text: Response uses contractions (don't, can't, it's, etc.)
expectations.evidence: "you've ruled out your own code" — 'you've' is a contraction present in the response.

expectations.text: Response uses short synonyms or fragment patterns typical of caveman mode
expectations.evidence: Response is entirely full formal prose: "Every time you call `emitter.on(...)` without a corresponding `removeListener`, you add a listener that holds a reference to its closure and any captured variables." No caveman fragments or shortened synonyms.

expectations.text: Covers at least 2 common causes of Node.js memory leaks
expectations.evidence: Covers 7 causes: event listener accumulation, unbounded caches/global maps, closures capturing large objects, timers and intervals never cleared, forgotten async operations/pending promises, streams not consumed or destroyed, third-party module leaks.

expectations.text: Mentions at least one concrete debugging tool or technique
expectations.evidence: Multiple tools: `--expose-gc` flag with `process.memoryUsage()`, `v8.writeHeapSnapshot()` and Chrome DevTools with `--inspect`, `clinic heap` package from NearForm, Node's MaxListenersExceededWarning.

expectations.text: Comments use the L<line>: format with file reference where applicable
expectations.evidence: Response uses section headings ("### `getUser` — Minor Style Issue", "### `deleteUser` — Several Issues") and numbered sub-points. No L<line>: format used anywhere.
